flashcards.front: What is an Entity in the E-R model?
flashcards.back: An entity is a real-world object or concept that exists independently and can be uniquely identified. Examples: Student, Employee, Product. It represents a 'thing' that can store data in a database.
An entity set is a collection of entities of the same type that share common attributes. For example, all students in a college form the 'Student' entity set.

flashcards.front: Define an Attribute in E-R modeling.
flashcards.back: An attribute is a characteristic or property of an entity that describes it. For example, a Student entity has attributes like Student_ID, Name, Age, and Grade.
A composite attribute can be divided into smaller sub-attributes. For example, 'Name' can be split into 'FirstName', 'MiddleName', and 'LastName'. It provides more flexibility in data storage.
A single-valued attribute holds only one value for a particular entity. For example, a person has only one 'Date_of_Birth' or 'Age' at any given time.
A multivalued attribute can have multiple values for a single entity. It is represented by double ovals in E-R diagrams. Examples: Phone_Numbers, Email_Addresses, Degrees.
A derived attribute's value can be calculated or derived from other related attributes. For example, 'Age' can be derived from 'Date_of_Birth'. Shown as dashed oval in E-R diagrams.
A key attribute uniquely identifies an entity within an entity set. It must have a unique value for each entity. Example: Student_ID, Employee_ID, ISBN. Represented as underlined in diagrams.

flashcards.front: Define a Relationship in E-R modeling.
flashcards.back: A relationship describes an association or connection between two or more entities. It represents how entities interact with each other. Examples: 'Enrolled_in', 'Works_for', 'Teaches'.
A relationship set is a collection of relationships of the same type. For example, all 'Teaches' relationships between teachers and courses form the 'Teaches' relationship set.
Cardinality specifies the number of instances of one entity that can associate with a single instance of another entity. Types: One-to-One (1:1), One-to-Many (1:N), Many-to-Many (M:N).

Total participation (existence dependency) means every entity in an entity set must participate in the relationship. Denoted by double line in E-R diagram. Example: Every student must enroll in a course.
Partial participation means an entity may or may not participate in the relationship. It's optional. Represented by a single line in E-R diagrams.
A weak entity is an entity that cannot be uniquely identified by its own attributes alone and depends on a strong entity for identification. It has a partial key. Represented by double rectangle.
A recursive relationship (also called unary) occurs when an entity relates to itself. Example: Employee manages Employee, where one employee reports to another employee.
